skill.name: firecrawl
skill.description: Use Firecrawl CLI instructions for web search, page scraping, site mapping, and extraction into LLM-friendly files.
skill.summary: Search, scrape, and map web content with the Firecrawl CLI.
skill.author: firecrawl

security_audit.summary: The static findings are partly true positives: this skill intentionally runs external CLI commands, writes files, accesses network resources, and handles Firecrawl API keys. I found no evidence of confirmed malicious exfiltration, but unsafe shell patterns, sudo installation guidance, credential persistence, and overbroad tool override language require remediation before publication.

high_findings.title: Shell Injection Risk in Parallel Scrape Pattern
high_findings.description: The xargs example substitutes URL data into sh -c and builds a filename with command substitution. A crafted URL list could break quoting and execute unintended shell syntax.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky pattern combines xargs, sh -c, placeholder substitution, and command substitution around externally sourced URLs. This is a concrete injection surface, even though it appears intended for batch scraping.

high_findings.title: Privileged Global Package Installation Guidance
high_findings.description: The troubleshooting section includes sudo npm install -g as an option. Running a package installer with elevated privileges can execute package scripts as root.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file labels the sudo option as not recommended, but still presents the command for use. The security impact of privileged npm installation is well understood.

high_findings.title: Overbroad Tool Override Language
high_findings.description: The skill says Firecrawl must replace built-in and third-party web tools for all internet tasks. This can override user or platform tool choices and force network use beyond the immediate need.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The language is explicit and uses mandatory wording such as no exceptions and must replace. It does not target the audit process, so I assess it as high risk rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: API Key Collection and Persistence Instructions
medium_findings.description: The installation rules ask the agent to collect a Firecrawl API key and suggest adding it to shell startup files. This can expose long-lived credentials if logs or profiles are shared.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential handling is clearly present and related to legitimate authentication. I found no evidence that the key is sent anywhere except Firecrawl CLI or environment configuration.

medium_findings.title: Broad External Command Execution by Design
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs agents to run firecrawl, npm, jq, grep, wc, xargs, and shell background jobs. This is expected for a CLI skill but increases operational risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented examples for the intended tool workflow. Most are not malicious by themselves, but they depend on safe argument handling and user authorization.

medium_findings.title: Local File Writes and Hidden Working Directory Use
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to create a hidden .firecrawl directory, store outputs, and use scratchpad scripts. This can leave scraped data or generated scripts in the project tree.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is explicit and supports the skill workflow. The risk is data retention and accidental inclusion in projects, not direct compromise.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Expected Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are example targets for scrape and map commands. They demonstrate expected network functionality and do not show credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are generic examples such as example.com and sample sites. No evidence found that secrets are appended to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The visible md5 usage is for deriving a local output filename in a batch scrape example, not for password storage, signatures, or security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The md5 reference appears only in a filename generation context. It is still embedded in an unsafe shell pattern, which is covered separately as a high finding.

dangerous_patterns.title: xargs with sh -c and Untrusted Placeholder Substitution
dangerous_patterns.description: The batch scrape example passes xargs data into a shell command string. This is dangerous when URLs or file names can contain quotes, substitutions, or separators.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is a known shell injection risk. It appears in executable instructions rather than inert prose.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo npm install -g
dangerous_patterns.description: The install guide includes a privileged npm install command. Package install scripts can run during installation with elevated privileges.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present in the troubleshooting steps. The surrounding text discourages it but does not remove the risky option.

dangerous_patterns.title: Long-Lived API Key in Shell Profile
dangerous_patterns.description: The manual authentication flow suggests exporting FIRECRAWL_API_KEY and adding it to a shell profile. Persistent secrets in profile files are easy to leak.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and involves a credential value. It is legitimate authentication, so the finding is about exposure risk rather than confirmed misuse.

content.user_title: Search and Extract Web Content with Firecrawl
content.value_statement: Web research often needs clean page content, search results, and site maps. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use Firecrawl CLI for structured web extraction.
actual_capabilities: Search web, news, and image sources with Firecrawl CLI options.
Scrape pages into markdown, HTML, links, screenshots, or JSON formats.
Map sites to discover URLs, subdomains, and filtered paths.
Store search and scrape outputs in a local .firecrawl directory.
Process saved results with command line tools such as jq and grep.
Guide browser login or API key authentication for Firecrawl CLI.
limitations: Requires Firecrawl CLI, a Firecrawl account, and available API credits.
Runs external commands and may write files into the current project.
Results depend on Firecrawl service availability and target site access.
Batch scraping examples need safer shell quoting before production use.

use_cases.title: Research Current Web Sources
use_cases.description: Find recent pages, scrape promising results, and save clean summaries for review.

use_cases.title: Extract Product Documentation
use_cases.description: Scrape documentation pages and map related URLs for implementation planning.

use_cases.title: Collect Competitive Site Data
use_cases.description: Map competitor pages and extract main content for structured comparison.

prompt_templates.title: Search a Topic
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the web for recent information about this topic and save the results with Firecrawl: [topic].

prompt_templates.title: Scrape One Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Firecrawl to scrape this page into clean markdown, then summarize the main points: [URL].

prompt_templates.title: Map a Documentation Site
prompt_templates.prompt: Map this documentation site with Firecrawl, identify relevant pages, and extract the sections about [feature]: [URL].

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Sources
prompt_templates.prompt: Search, scrape, and compare sources about [question]. Save outputs, cite source pages, and flag conflicts between sources.

output_examples.input: Search for recent announcements about a software framework.
output_examples.output: A saved search file, a short ranked source list, and a concise summary of the most relevant announcements.
A clean markdown extraction with main content, key headings, links found on the page, and a summary for implementation.

output_examples.input: Map a company website for pricing and case study pages.
output_examples.output: A filtered URL list grouped by page type, with recommended pages to scrape next.

best_practices: Confirm the target domain and scope before running broad crawl or map commands.
Save outputs outside tracked source files or keep .firecrawl in .gitignore.
Review commands that include user URLs before using parallel shell execution.
anti_patterns: Do not scrape sensitive, private, or login-protected pages without clear authorization.
Do not paste API keys into prompts, logs, or shared chat transcripts.
Do not run shell batch examples on untrusted URL lists without sanitizing input.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Firecrawl account?
faq.answer: Yes. It expects Firecrawl CLI authentication through browser login or an API key.

faq.question: Can it search news and images?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented search command supports web, news, and image sources.

faq.question: Where are outputs stored?
faq.answer: The skill recommends storing generated files under a local .firecrawl directory.

faq.question: Does it run external commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It is built around Firecrawl CLI and related shell utilities.

faq.question: Is it safe for private data?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill sends URLs and scraping requests to an external Firecrawl service.

faq.question: Why is publication not recommended yet?
faq.answer: The audit found unsafe shell examples, sudo install guidance, credential persistence, and overbroad tool override language.
